title: Dog Hair Loss in Circular Patches: Causes and What to Do
meta_desc: Find out why your dog has circular hair loss. Learn about ringworm, skin infections, and why this low-urgency issue is best triaged with a photo.
content_html:
Understanding Circular Hair Loss in Dogs

Finding circular patches of missing fur on your dog can be concerning, but please know that this is a very common issue that veterinarians see regularly. These distinct, coin-sized spots are often caused by ringworm (a fungal infection), bacterial skin infections, or even mites. While it can look uncomfortable, it is usually a localized skin condition rather than a sign of a life-threatening illness.

Is It an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. In the vast majority of cases, circular hair loss is not an emergency. If your dog is otherwise acting like themselves—eating well, staying active, and not in visible distress—you can typically wait for the next available appointment with your primary veterinarian. However, if the area is oozing, bleeding, or if your dog is scratching so intensely that they are causing self-injury, you should seek care sooner to provide them with some relief.

The Power of a Photo in Triage

When you are dealing with skin issues, a clear, high-resolution photo is one of the most valuable tools you can provide. A photograph allows a veterinary professional to see the exact color, texture, and borders of the hair loss. It helps us differentiate between a simple dry patch and something more infectious like ringworm. Additionally, taking a photo today gives you a baseline to track whether the patches are growing or spreading while you wait for your appointment.

faqs.question: Is ringworm contagious to humans?
faqs.answer: Yes, ringworm is a fungal infection that can be transmitted from dogs to humans and other pets. It is important to wash your hands after touching the affected areas.

faqs.question: Does circular hair loss always mean my dog has parasites?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. While mites or fungal infections are common, circular patches can also be caused by localized bacterial infections or even certain hormonal imbalances.

faqs.question: Should I put anti-itch cream on the spots?
faqs.answer: You should consult your vet before applying any creams. Some human medications can be toxic if licked off, and others may interfere with the diagnostic tests your vet needs to perform.
